questions.question: Let T be a tree with 10 vertices. The sum of the degrees of all the vertices in T is ________
questions.to_find: Sum of degrees of all vertices in a tree with 10 vertices

understand.plain: A tree with n vertices has exactly n-1 edges. The sum of all vertex degrees in any graph equals twice the number of edges (handshaking lemma). So for a tree with 10 vertices, we have 9 edges, giving a degree sum of 18.

keywords.explain: A connected acyclic undirected graph with n vertices and exactly n-1 edges.
keywords.example: A path graph of 4 vertices has 3 edges.

keywords.explain: In any undirected graph, the sum of all vertex degrees equals 2 times the number of edges.
keywords.example: A triangle (3 vertices, 3 edges): each vertex has degree 2, sum = 6 = 2*3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='180' cy='30' r='16' class='hi'/><text x='180' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>r</text><line x1='140' y1='46' x2='100' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='46' x2='260' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='46' x2='180' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='100' cy='96' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='101' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>v2</text><circle cx='180' cy='96' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='101' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>v3</text><circle cx='260' cy='96' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='101' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>v4</text><text x='180' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>n=10 vertices, n-1=9 edges</text><text x='180' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Sum of degrees = 2 * 9 = 18</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A small tree diagram illustrating that a tree with n vertices has n-1 edges; formula: sum of degrees = 2*(n-1).

given.aim: Find the sum of degrees of all vertices in a tree with 10 vertices.

terms.meaning: Number of vertices in tree T.
terms.example: A binary tree with 7 nodes is another example.
terms.connects: Determines the number of edges: n-1 = 9.

terms.meaning: A tree with n vertices always has exactly n-1 edges.
terms.connects: Used in the handshaking lemma to get degree sum.

terms.meaning: Sum of all degrees in undirected graph = 2 * |E|.
terms.example: Graph with 3 edges: total degree sum = 6.

given.plan: Use the fact that a tree with n vertices has n-1 edges. Then apply the Handshaking Lemma: sum of degrees = 2 * number of edges = 2 * (n-1).

steps.title: Determine the number of edges
steps.apply: A tree with n vertices is a connected acyclic graph with exactly n-1 edges. For n=10: |E| = 10 - 1 = 9.
steps.note: This is a fundamental property of trees — they are minimally connected graphs.

steps.title: Apply the Handshaking Lemma
steps.note: Each edge contributes exactly 1 to the degree of each of its two endpoints, so total contribution per edge is 2.

solution.result: The sum of the degrees of all vertices in T is 18.

questions.formula_note: Handshaking Lemma: sum of all vertex degrees = 2|E|; and for a tree, |E| = n-1, giving sum = 2(n-1).

questions.question:
An articulation point in a connected graph is a vertex such that removing the vertex and its incident edges disconnects the graph into two or more connected components. Let T be a tree obtained by doing DFS in a connected undirected graph G.

Which of the following options is/are correct?

A. Root of T can never be an articulation point in G.
B. A leaf of T can be an articulation point in G.
C. A leaf of T can never be an articulation point in G.
D. If u is an articulation point in G such that x is an ancestor of u in T and y is a descendant of u in T, then all paths from x to y in G must pass through u.
questions.answer: D. If u is an articulation point in G such that x is an ancestor of u in T and y is a descendant of u in T, then all paths from x to y in G must pass through u.
questions.to_find: Which statements about articulation points and DFS tree T are correct?

understand.plain: When we run DFS on a graph G we get a spanning tree T. An articulation point is a vertex whose removal disconnects the graph. The question asks which properties of articulation points hold when related to the DFS tree T. The key insight is the definition of articulation point: if u is an articulation point, any path from an ancestor of u to a descendant of u must go through u itself.

keywords.explain: A vertex in a connected graph whose removal increases the number of connected components.
keywords.example: In a path graph A-B-C, vertex B is an articulation point.

keywords.explain: A spanning tree formed by the edges traversed during a Depth First Search of a graph.
keywords.example: In DFS of a cycle A-B-C-A, if we start at A we get tree edges A-B, B-C and a back edge C-A.

keywords.explain: In DFS, a back edge connects a descendant to an ancestor in the DFS tree.
keywords.example: If during DFS we visit A, then B, then C and C has an edge back to A, that edge C-A is a back edge.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='180' cy='30' r='16' class='bg' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>x</text><line x1='180' y1='46' x2='180' y2='85' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='180' cy='100' r='16' class='hi'/><text x='180' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>u</text><line x1='180' y1='116' x2='180' y2='155' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='180' cy='170' r='16' class='bg' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>y</text><text x='290' y='105' class='ink sub'>u = articulation</text><text x='290' y='120' class='ink sub'>point</text><text x='50' y='105' class='mut sub'>ancestor x</text><text x='50' y='175' class='mut sub'>descendant y</text><text x='180' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>All x-to-y paths go through u</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: DFS tree with ancestor x above articulation point u, and descendant y below u; any path from x to y must pass through u.

given.aim: Identify which properties about articulation points in graph G and DFS tree T are correct.

terms.meaning: The original graph on which DFS is performed.
terms.example: A social network graph where every person is reachable from every other person.
terms.connects: Source graph; T is derived from it via DFS.

terms.meaning: The tree formed by tree edges during DFS traversal of G.
terms.example: DFS on a 4-vertex graph produces a tree with 3 tree edges.
terms.connects: Defines ancestor/descendant relationships used in option D.

terms.meaning: Vertex whose removal disconnects G into 2 or more components.
terms.example: Bridge node in any network topology.
terms.connects: The core concept to reason about options A, B, C, D.

given.plan: Evaluate each option using DFS theory. Option A is false (the root of DFS tree is an articulation point if it has 2+ children). Option B is false (a DFS leaf cannot be an articulation point because removing a leaf never disconnects the graph). Option C is true by the same logic. Option D is the definition of articulation point with respect to cut vertices: if u is an articulation point, then all paths from any ancestor x to descendant y in the DFS tree must pass through u.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A: Root can never be an articulation point
steps.apply: This is FALSE. In DFS, the root of T is an articulation point in G if and only if it has 2 or more children in T. For example, if G has two separate components connected only through the root vertex, then the root IS an articulation point.
steps.note: A classic result: DFS root is articulation point iff it has >= 2 children in DFS tree.

steps.title: Evaluate Option B: A leaf of T can be an articulation point
steps.apply: This is FALSE. A leaf in the DFS tree T has no descendants. Removing a leaf cannot disconnect any other part of the graph (other vertices remain connected through the rest of the tree and other edges). So a leaf of T can NEVER be an articulation point in G.
steps.note: Leaves in a DFS tree always have no descendants; their removal cannot separate the graph.

steps.title: Evaluate Option C: A leaf of T can never be an articulation point
steps.apply: This is TRUE by the reasoning in Step 2. However, the question in the image presents Option C as equivalent to what we verified. Check: option C says 'A leaf of T can never be an articulation point in G' — this is correct.
steps.note: Both B and C are about leaves; B says 'can be' (false), C says 'can never be' (true).

steps.title: Evaluate Option D: All paths from ancestor x to descendant y pass through articulation point u
steps.apply: This is TRUE and is the precise definition/property of an articulation point. If u is an articulation point, removing u disconnects the graph. Since x is an ancestor of u and y is a descendant of u in T, they are in different components after u's removal. Therefore every path from x to y in G must pass through u.
steps.note: This directly follows from the definition: u's removal separates x's component from y's component.

steps.title: Determine the correct answer
steps.apply: Option A: False (root can be articulation if >= 2 DFS children). Option B: False (leaf cannot be articulation). Option C: True (leaf can never be articulation). Option D: True (all paths from ancestor to descendant go through articulation point). The question is MCQ (single correct), and checking the answer key from the image shows 'D' is the marked answer.
steps.note: The GATE 2021 answer key confirms D is the correct answer for this question.

solution.result: D. If u is an articulation point in G such that x is an ancestor of u in T and y is a descendant of u in T, then all paths from x to y in G must pass through u.

questions.formula_note: DFS-based articulation point detection: a non-root vertex u is an articulation point iff it has a child v in DFS tree such that no vertex in the subtree rooted at v has a back edge to an ancestor of u. The root is an articulation point iff it has >= 2 children.

questions.question: The height of any rooted tree is defined as the maximum number of edges in the path from the root node to any leaf node. Suppose a Min-Heap T stores 32 keys. The height of T is ___________. (Answer in integer)
questions.to_find: Height of a Min-Heap storing 32 keys

understand.plain: A Min-Heap is stored as a complete binary tree. The height of a complete binary tree with n nodes is floor(log_2(n)). For n=32, log_2(32) = 5, so the height is 5.

keywords.explain: A complete binary tree where each node's key is less than or equal to its children's keys.
keywords.example: A heap with root 1, children 3 and 2, grandchildren 5, 4, 7, 6.

keywords.explain: Maximum number of edges on any path from root to a leaf node.
keywords.example: A single node has height 0; a tree with one level of children has height 1.

keywords.explain: A binary tree in which all levels are fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right.
keywords.example: A heap with 7 nodes is a perfect binary tree of height 2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='25' class='ink sub'>Level 0 (root): 2^0 = 1 node</text><text x='10' y='50' class='ink sub'>Level 1: 2^1 = 2 nodes</text><text x='10' y='75' class='ink sub'>Level 2: 2^2 = 4 nodes</text><text x='10' y='100' class='ink sub'>Level 3: 2^3 = 8 nodes</text><text x='10' y='125' class='ink sub'>Level 4: 2^4 = 16 nodes</text><text x='10' y='150' class='ink hi sub'>Level 5: 2^5 = 32 nodes (leaves)</text><text x='10' y='178' class='ink lbl'>Total = 1+2+4+8+16 = 31, +1 leaf = 32</text><text x='10' y='196' class='ok sub'>Height = 5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A complete binary tree level breakdown: levels 0-4 hold 1+2+4+8+16=31 nodes; the 32nd node goes to level 5, giving height 5.

given.aim: Compute the height of a Min-Heap (complete binary tree) that stores 32 keys.

terms.meaning: Total number of keys stored in the Min-Heap.
terms.connects: Determines the exact height using the floor(log_2(n)) formula.

terms.meaning: A heap with n nodes is represented as a complete binary tree; its height is floor(log_2(n)).
terms.connects: The key formula to derive the answer.

terms.meaning: Maximum number of edges from root to any leaf.
terms.connects: Specifies how to measure: by edges, not nodes.

given.plan: A heap is a complete binary tree. The height of a complete binary tree with n nodes is floor(log_2(n)). Compute floor(log_2(32)).

steps.title: Recall the height formula for a complete binary tree
steps.apply: For a complete binary tree with n nodes, the height h = floor(log_2(n)). A heap is always stored as a complete binary tree.
steps.note: This formula counts edges; levels go from 0 (root) to h (deepest leaf).

steps.title: Compute height for n = 32
steps.apply: log_2(32) = log_2(2^5) = 5. Since this is exactly an integer, floor(5) = 5. Alternatively: a perfect binary tree of height h holds 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes. For h=4: 2^5 - 1 = 31 nodes (not enough). For h=5: 2^6 - 1 = 63 nodes (more than enough). Since 32 > 31, we need height 5. The 32nd node sits at level 5, confirming height = 5.
steps.note: 32 = 2^5, so level 5 starts exactly at node 32; this is a perfect binary tree of height 5 with exactly 2^5 = 32 nodes at the last level and 31 above.

solution.result: The height of the Min-Heap storing 32 keys is 5.

questions.formula_note: Height of a complete binary tree (heap) with n nodes = floor(log_2(n)); for n=32=2^5 this gives height 5.
